topics.headline: California's new $5B one-time block grant explicitly includes charter schools — and the $3.9B Newsom withhold could shrink it
topics.why_matters: The Legislature's budget includes $1 billion more for community schools, a $2.4 billion boost to ongoing special education funding, and a $5 billion one-time block grant that districts and charter schools can spend however they want. That last clause is the headline for every Tier A partner — Visions, iLEAD, Sage Oak, Heartland and the rest get unrestricted dollars they can route into intervention. At the same time, the $3.9 billion in education funding that Newsom would withhold until revenue projections come true is being contested, with education groups threatening to sue over the delay. The compliance window is short and the funding picture is unstable — exactly when charter directors need someone telling them what to do with the money.
topics.angle: "California charter schools just got a $5B discretionary block grant. Here are three intervention investments that defend the spend in your next audit." Lead with the LAO/EdSource numbers, anchor to A+'s ESSA-aligned model.
topics.roman_take: The discretionary block grant is a trap if you treat it like found money — districts that spend it on one-time tech licenses will be apologizing to their boards in 18 months. Conviction angle: only evidence-tied spending survives.
topics.danielle_take: Practical implementation playbook — how a charter director should layer the $5B block grant on top of Title I, Title III, and LCFF supplemental/concentration without supplanting. Reference the CARS calendar and audit timing. ---

topics.headline: iLEAD Schools launches iLEAD Flex in Lancaster — first new charter post-NCB-moratorium
topics.why_matters: iLEAD — one of A+'s largest Tier A partner networks — announced June 18 that it is expanding its charter network into Lancaster, opening iLEAD Flex as a new TK-12 campus, and explicitly framed the move as a response to the statewide moratorium on new non-classroom-based charters concluding on January 1, 2026. iLEAD Flex is expected to open with approximately 750 learners, bringing iLEAD's total network enrollment to nearly 7,000 students, and is part of a longer growth plan that includes iLEAD Innovate — the network's first school outside Los Angeles County — expected to open fall 2027. A+ already serves iLEAD Hybrid, Antelope Valley, Lancaster, Exploration, and Online. A new Lancaster site = new intervention contracts, new EL/SPED populations to onboard, and a public moment where A+ can celebrate a partner.
topics.angle: Congratulate iLEAD on iLEAD Flex Lancaster and use it to spotlight what happens when flex-based charters scale: intervention infrastructure has to scale with them, or new students inherit old service gaps.
topics.roman_take: The NCB moratorium lift isn't just a policy footnote — it's the starting gun for the next decade of independent-study charter growth in CA, and the schools that win will be the ones that built intervention systems BEFORE they scaled.
topics.danielle_take: When a partner opens a new campus, the intervention conversation has to start before day one — by the time CAASPP scores come back showing the gap, you've already lost a year of Title I/III leverage; here's the 90-day onboarding checklist that prevents that. ---

topics.headline: Catapult Learning makes "the tutor–student relationship" its lead HIT message — and walks straight into A+'s lane
topics.why_matters: Catapult Learning, a division of FullBloom, announced a free professional development webinar — "The Human Element: Why the Tutor-Student Relationship Drives Tutoring Outcomes" — held June 22, 2026. Their VP of Teaching and Learning framed the pitch this way: high-impact tutoring is often discussed in terms of dosage, curriculum and data, but relationships are what bring those elements to life. The panel was anchored by Liz Cohen, VP of policy at 50CAN and author of The Future of Tutoring — meaning the most influential HIT policy voice in the country just legitimized the relational frame on a competitor's stage. Catapult Learning partners with 500+ school districts, so this is not a small competitor talking — it is the largest at-scale K-12 intervention provider repositioning its message around the differentiator A+ has always led with. If A+ doesn't plant a flag here in the next 7–10 days, A+ cedes the relational frame to the player with 500-district reach.
topics.angle: "Relationships have always been the lever. Welcome to the conversation — here's what 13 years of doing it actually looks like." Use a partner-tutor pairing data point (sessions per tutor-student dyad, retention rate of tutor matches) to show A+ has the receipts, not just the talking point.
topics.roman_take: When the biggest player in your category starts using your language, that's not validation — it's a warning that the differentiator is about to commoditize unless you defend it with proof points only you have.
topics.danielle_take: Webinars about relationships are easy; staffing model design that actually produces consistent tutor-student dyads inside a charter's master schedule is the hard part. Here's the operational checklist directors should ask any vendor — including A+. ---
